blocks.content: Eighth grade started the way things started when you already knew how they worked. No first-day nerves, no recalibration — just the same building, one year heavier, with the understanding that this was the last year before everything changed again.

Kit had spent most of the summer not thinking about Brooke Hadley and thinking about her anyway. They'd talked on AIM through July and August — nothing serious, nothing named — and by the time school started again, the thing between them had the shape of something without being called anything. She sat at their lunch table now without it being a question. She and Daisy were friendly in the way that two people were friendly when they understood each other perfectly and didn't need to discuss it.
Daisy hadn't said anything about Brooke since April, which was either fine or not fine, and Kit had made the choice not to find out which.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: Presidential campaign. Year two. I need a slogan.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: You already won. Why do you need a slogan?

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: Incumbents need slogans more than challengers. It's about maintaining momentum.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: How about: still here, still Eli.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: I love that. I'm using that.
Kit watched Daisy laugh at that and looked back at his lunch and thought: fine. Everything was fine.
